cases.title: Family A: Recurrent Molecular Frame
cases.summary: A structural frame with restricted slot variation, observed repeatedly across folios.
cases.shows: A frozen ATOMS structural frame reappears across annotated folios with restricted slot values.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not prove semantic meaning, word identity, or that the frame is predictive beyond local syntax.
cases.verification: Inspect the frozen Family A documentation and GRAMMAR-V1 tables.
highlights: The same skeleton with restricted slot choices appears in all five annotated folios.
No new slot values appeared in held-out or prospective folios.
This pattern is structural, not claimed as a word or morpheme.

cases.title: f3r Supportive Region: ATOMS vs EVA
cases.summary: A region where ATOMS predicted better than EVA under frozen local-context metrics.
cases.shows: In this reported region, the frozen local-context protocol assigned lower uncertainty to ATOMS than EVA.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not prove global ATOMS superiority or decipherment.
cases.verification: Inspect the prospective model results table.
highlights: ATOMS normalized log-loss: 0.21 vs EVA: 0.64 in this region.
The model had seen similar ATOMS local contexts in training.
EVA saw an unfamiliar token shape in this position.

cases.title: img7-m50: Boundary Continuity Candidate
cases.summary: A molecule where observed structure suggests particle flow may continue across molecule boundaries.
cases.shows: A concrete case that motivates testing whether ATOMS flow can cross molecule boundaries.
cases.doesNotShow: It is not an established rule and should not be counted as demonstrated grammar.
cases.verification: Inspect the anomaly ledger and future boundary-continuity notes.
cases.dominantNote: Dominant molecule-local would be: [c:1] [f:1 j:1] [n:1] [e:1 g:1 e:1]
highlights: If we look only at molecule-local order, m50 looks like a rareza (scrambled).
But the previous molecule ends with c:1 | f:1 j:1, so the particle flow may continue.
This is a testable hypothesis, not a result. See research/notes/BOUNDARY-CONTINUITY-SEEDS.md

cases.title: ATOMS Strong Win Region
cases.summary: A region where ATOMS dramatically outperformed EVA.
cases.shows: A strong regional ATOMS win under the published regional comparison protocol.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not imply every region favors ATOMS or that EVA is invalid.
cases.verification: Inspect the regional comparison report and model table.
highlights: ATOMS preserves the local particle structure that EVA linearizes.
The ATOMS advantage is largest in regions with complex particle composition.
EVA compresses the sequence into a single token, losing internal structure.

cases.title: EVA Competitive Region
cases.summary: A region where EVA performed nearly as well as ATOMS.
cases.relevance: EVA is not useless; some structure is shared
cases.shows: Some common short forms remain competitive under EVA.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not erase the broader regional ATOMS advantage reported elsewhere.
cases.verification: Inspect the ablation report and regional score tables.
highlights: In short, common sequences, EVA captures similar structure.
This is expected: frequent EVA tokens also have regular ATOMS composition.
The ATOMS advantage is largest on longer, more varied sequences.

cases.title: Anomaly Confirmed as Valid Annotation
cases.summary: A visually confirmed labeling that initially appeared unusual.
cases.shows: Automated anomaly flags can be reviewed and preserved as known-valid cases.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not independently validate the entire corpus or replace second-annotator review.
cases.verification: Inspect the known anomalies TSV and audit script.
highlights: Atom 5493 was flagged by automated audit as a potential labeling anomaly.
Manual visual inspection confirmed the label is correct.
The case was added to known-labeling-anomalies.tsv as known-valid.
This prevents the audit from re-flagging it, without hiding the record.

cases.title: Atom g:1: High-Frequency Right-Curve Gallows
cases.summary: g:1 is the most structurally interesting frequent atom due to its positional rigidity.
cases.relevance: Shows how individual atoms carry positional information
cases.shows: A frequent ATOMS symbol with unusually concentrated positional behavior.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not assign a phonetic, semantic, or morphemic value to g:1.
cases.verification: Inspect the Corpus V2 math report.
highlights: g:1 appears almost exclusively in a specific position within molecules.
Its positional entropy is among the lowest of all ATOMS symbols.
This suggests g:1 may serve a specific structural role, not random placement.
This is a structural observation, not a semantic claim.

cases.title: Molecule e:1 g:1 e:1: A Recurring Triplet
cases.summary: One of the most common ATOMS molecule patterns, spanning all folios.
cases.shows: A stable recurring ATOMS triplet visible across the audited corpus.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not establish that the triplet is a word, morpheme, or translation unit.
cases.verification: Inspect the Corpus V2 molecules table.
highlights: This triplet appears dozens of times across all annotated folios.
Its internal structure (e:1 g:1 e:1) is stable: e:1 bookends a g:1.
It can appear as a standalone molecule or embedded in longer sequences.
e:1 is the most frequent atom overall (1,496 occurrences).

cases.title: Clean 1:1 Alignment Region
cases.summary: A region where ATOMS particles and EVA tokens align one-to-one.
cases.shows: A clean alignment case supporting the regional comparison method.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not imply all manuscript regions align cleanly.
highlights: Each ATOMS particle maps cleanly to an EVA token in this region.
This is the ideal case for the regional alignment method.
About 93% of regions aligned without issues.
15 of 224 total regions remain unresolved.

cases.title: Null Control V2: Local Context Explains Much
cases.summary: A null model using local context reproduced much of the apparent grammar specificity.
cases.relevance: Negative result that weakened a stronger grammar claim
cases.shows: Local context explains much of the apparent GRAMMAR-V1 slot compatibility.
cases.doesNotShow: It does not refute all ATOMS structure; it narrows the grammar claim.
cases.verification: Inspect the Null Control V2 report.
highlights: Contextual empirical probability was approximately 0.099.
This means local syntax explains much of the apparent slot compatibility.
The exact frame identity claim was weakened, not refuted completely.
This result is published alongside supportive results.
